metadata.description: Held-out negotiation active_listening pairs for probing only. NOT used in vector extraction. Same domain (negotiation) as extraction pairs but entirely new scenarios. Tests whether the active_listening vector generalises within negotiation contexts.

dimensions.name: Active Listening
dimensions.description: Demonstrating genuine attention by reflecting back, summarising, clarifying what was said before responding

pairs.context: A client has just explained a long list of concerns about a proposed contract, covering timelines, payment terms, and liability clauses.
pairs.positive: Let me make sure I've understood correctly — you have three main concerns: the timeline feels too compressed, the payment schedule front-loads too much risk for you, and the liability cap is lower than you are comfortable with. Is that an accurate summary?
pairs.negative: We can be flexible on a few things. The liability cap is standard in our contracts but we might have room on the payment terms. What is most important to you?

pairs.context: During a salary negotiation, a candidate has explained why they are asking for a higher number, citing recent work they did that went beyond their job description.
pairs.positive: So if I understand you correctly, the core of your ask isn't just the number itself — it's that you've been doing work at the next level for some time now and want the title and compensation to match that reality. Is that right?
pairs.negative: I hear you on the additional contributions. Let me take that back to HR and see what we can do on the number.

pairs.context: A vendor is explaining why there was a delay in delivery, mentioning supply chain issues, a staff illness, and a miscommunication with logistics.
pairs.positive: So there were three separate things that combined here — a supply issue you didn't control, a team member being ill at a critical moment, and a communication gap with logistics. Is that the full picture?
pairs.negative: I understand there were issues on your side. What I need to know is what the new delivery date is and how you plan to prevent this happening again.

pairs.context: A co-founder is explaining why they think the company should pivot and has given multiple reasons including market feedback, competitive pressure, and their personal conviction.
pairs.positive: It sounds like the pivot isn't just about one data point — you're seeing the market signal, feeling the competitive squeeze, and on a personal level you believe in this direction more than the current one. Have I got that right?
pairs.negative: I hear you on the market side. I'm not sure I agree on the competitive angle, but let's talk through the data before we make a decision.

pairs.context: A buyer is explaining why they can't go above a certain price and has mentioned budget constraints, board approval limits, and a competing quote.
pairs.positive: Just so I understand your position fully — you have a hard ceiling because of board approval, not just preference, and you also have a competing quote that is below my number. Is there anything else shaping your ceiling that I should understand?
pairs.negative: If budget is the issue, let's look at what we can take out of scope to bring the price down. What features could you live without?

pairs.context: A supplier is explaining that quality issues arose because a component they source from a third party was substandard, and they have since changed supplier.
pairs.positive: So the root cause was a third-party component, not your manufacturing process, and you've already addressed it by changing supplier. What I want to understand is whether the new supplier has been verified or if this is still being tested.
pairs.negative: Understood. What guarantees can you put in the contract to ensure quality standards going forward?

pairs.context: An employee in a redundancy consultation has explained that they are not just worried about money but about their professional identity and what they will do next.
pairs.positive: What I'm hearing is that the financial concern, while real, isn't the only thing — you're also grappling with what losing this role means for who you are professionally. That's a very different kind of conversation to have. Is that right?
pairs.negative: I understand this is difficult. The package includes a notice period and redundancy pay. We can also look at what outplacement support we can offer.

pairs.context: A negotiating counterpart has objected to a clause and given a detailed explanation involving past experience with a similar contract that went badly.
pairs.positive: Before I respond to the clause itself, I want to make sure I understand your experience — you've been in a situation where this type of clause was used against you in a way that caused real damage, and that's informing your position now. Is that the core of it?
pairs.negative: I understand you've had bad experiences with similar language. Our clause is actually more limited in scope — let me walk you through how it differs.

pairs.context: A client is pushing back on the project timeline and mentions several reasons including internal resource constraints and an upcoming board presentation.
pairs.positive: So the timeline pressure is coming from two directions — you don't have the internal bandwidth to keep pace, and there's a fixed board date that is creating a hard deadline. Which of those two is the more immovable constraint?
pairs.negative: We can look at ways to accelerate delivery. The main lever is usually scope — if we reduce what's included in phase one, we can hit an earlier date.

pairs.context: A potential partner explains they are hesitant about the deal because of concerns about your company's financial stability after a recent round of layoffs.
pairs.positive: I want to make sure I understand your concern — the layoffs made you question whether we have the runway to see this partnership through to maturity. Is that the underlying worry, or is there more to it?
pairs.negative: The layoffs were a strategic restructuring, not a sign of financial distress. I'm happy to walk you through our current financial position.

pairs.context: A landlord is explaining their reluctance to lower the rent and mentions the mortgage rate, maintenance costs, and what other properties in the area are fetching.
pairs.positive: If I've followed you correctly, you're dealing with three cost pressures — higher mortgage repayments, ongoing maintenance, and a market that could support a higher rate. So lowering the rent creates a real problem for your numbers, not just your preference. Do I have that right?
pairs.negative: I appreciate the transparency. The issue is that comparable properties are actually coming in lower on the market right now, so I'd like to revisit the comparable you're using.

pairs.context: A prospective client has explained at length that they had a difficult experience with a previous agency and want guarantees before committing.
pairs.positive: I want to check I've understood what you need — it's not just about the outputs we promise, it's about the process and the communication, because that's where things broke down last time. You want to know how we work, not just what we deliver.
pairs.negative: Bad experiences with previous agencies are unfortunately common. I can give you case studies and references from clients who have worked with us long term.

pairs.context: An investor is expressing concern about a high valuation and mentions market comparables, the company's early stage, and a risk premium they factor in.
pairs.positive: So your concern is three-layered — the comparables don't support this valuation, early-stage companies carry more execution risk, and you apply a specific risk discount that puts you below our number. Is that a fair summary of where you are?
pairs.negative: Our comparables are different because of the IP and the market position. I'm happy to go through the model with you to show how we arrived at the number.

pairs.context: During a contract renegotiation, the other side explains they need better payment terms, citing cash flow issues that are seasonal in their business.
pairs.positive: So the cash flow pressure is cyclical — it's not a permanent problem but a seasonal one, and you need the payment schedule to account for that pattern. Is the ask about moving when payments fall, or about the total amounts?
pairs.negative: Seasonal cash flow is a common issue. We could look at extended payment terms or a structured payment plan. What would work best for you?

pairs.context: A client explains they are unhappy with the results of a project and cites several issues including unclear briefs, slow feedback loops, and final outputs that missed the mark.
pairs.positive: So there were failures at multiple stages — the brief wasn't clear enough at the start, the feedback cycle was too slow to catch problems early, and the end result didn't land where it needed to. I want to understand which of those you see as most attributable to us versus a shared failure.
pairs.negative: I understand you're unhappy with the outcome. Let's go through the project timeline and identify exactly where things went wrong so we can agree what remediation looks like.
